name: Physical Education
description: Foundational concepts in physical fitness, exercise science, and lifelong wellness habits. Students explore the five components of fitness, movement skills, sport participation, and healthy lifestyle design.

modules.title: Introduction to Physical Fitness
modules.objectives: Define physical fitness; identify its five health-related components; understand why each matters for lifelong health.
modules.assignment: Assess your current fitness level using all 5 components. Create a personal fitness goal statement and a one-week exercise plan.

modules.title: The FITT Principle
modules.objectives: Explain Frequency, Intensity, Time, and Type; apply the FITT principle to design a safe and effective personal workout program.
modules.readingNote: ACE Fitness — What Is the FITT Principle?
modules.video2Note: Applying FITT to Different Fitness Goals
modules.assignment: Design a 4-week workout program using the FITT principle targeting at least two fitness components. Justify each variable choice.

modules.title: Cardiovascular Endurance
modules.objectives: Define cardiovascular endurance; explain heart and lung responses to aerobic exercise; identify activities that build aerobic fitness.
modules.video2Note: How Aerobic Exercise Improves Heart Health
modules.assignment: Track your resting heart rate for 5 days. Complete 3 aerobic sessions; record distance, time, and perceived exertion after each.

modules.title: Muscular Strength and Endurance
modules.objectives: Distinguish muscular strength from muscular endurance; identify major muscle groups; describe resistance training principles for each quality.
modules.assignment: Perform a push-up, sit-up, and wall-sit test. Record results and compare to age norms. Write a 4-week strength plan for one major muscle group.

modules.title: Flexibility and Stretching
modules.objectives: Define flexibility; distinguish static, dynamic, and PNF stretching; explain how regular stretching reduces injury risk.
modules.readingNote: ACE Fitness — The Science and Practice of Stretching
modules.assignment: Perform a sit-and-reach flexibility test. Develop a 10-minute daily stretching routine. Record range-of-motion improvements over one week.

modules.title: Body Composition
modules.objectives: Define body composition; explain lean mass vs. fat mass; describe healthy body fat ranges and assessment methods including BMI limitations.
modules.practiceNote: ACE Fitness — BMI and Body Composition Calculators
modules.assignment: Calculate your BMI and research your healthy body fat range. Discuss how exercise and diet affect body composition in a one-page reflection.

modules.title: Goal Setting and Fitness Plans
modules.objectives: Apply the SMART goal framework to fitness goals; build a structured personal training plan with measurable outcomes and timelines.
modules.readingNote: CDC — Adding Physical Activity to Your Life
modules.video2Note: How to Create a Fitness Plan
modules.practiceNote: ACE Fitness — SMART Goal Setting Guide
modules.assignment: Write 3 SMART fitness goals (short-, medium-, and long-term). Build an 8-week training calendar addressing each goal with specific workouts.

modules.title: Nutrition for Active People
modules.objectives: Identify macronutrients and their roles in exercise; explain pre- and post-workout nutrition strategies; apply hydration guidelines.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Energy in Our Food
modules.assignment: Keep a 3-day food diary. Analyze macronutrient intake using a tracker. Write recommendations to improve your pre-workout nutrition.

modules.title: Injury Prevention and Safety
modules.objectives: Identify common exercise injuries; explain warm-up, cool-down, and proper technique as prevention strategies; describe the R.I.C.E. protocol.
modules.videoNote: Common Sports Injuries and How to Prevent Them
modules.practiceNote: ACE Fitness — How to Prevent Common Exercise Injuries
modules.assignment: Create a one-page injury prevention plan for a sport of your choice. Include warm-up, cool-down, technique tips, and equipment checklist.

modules.title: Team Sports Fundamentals
modules.objectives: Describe rules, positions, and basic strategies for at least two team sports; explain the value of teamwork and sportsmanship.
modules.assignment: Participate in two team sport activities. Write a 300-word analysis of teamwork dynamics and your personal contribution and sportsmanship.

modules.title: Individual Sports and Lifetime Activities
modules.titleZh: 个人运动与终身体育活动
modules.objectives: Identify a variety of individual sports and lifetime physical activities; explain how participation can be maintained safely into adulthood.
modules.readingNote: CDC — Physical Activity Guidelines for Adults
modules.assignment: Try one individual sport or lifetime activity new to you. Reflect on skills needed, personal challenges, and how you might continue participating.

modules.title: Mental Health and Exercise
modules.objectives: Explain psychological benefits of regular physical activity; describe exercise's relationship to stress, anxiety, and depression; apply findings to personal wellness.
modules.readingNote: CDC — Physical Activity and Mental Health
modules.assignment: Complete a 2-week exercise log tracking physical output and mood daily. Write a reflective essay analyzing how exercise affected your mental wellbeing.

modules.title: Fitness Assessment and Progress Tracking
modules.objectives: Administer standard fitness tests; interpret results against age-group norms; set data-driven improvement goals and re-test schedules.
modules.videoNote: How to Conduct a Fitness Assessment
modules.assignment: Complete a full fitness assessment battery. Record results in a data table, compare to norms, and create a 6-week improvement plan with re-test targets.

modules.title: Creating a Personal Wellness Lifestyle
modules.titleZh: 建立个人健康生活方式
modules.objectives: Synthesize course concepts into a sustainable personal wellness plan; identify community and digital resources that support long-term health.
modules.video2Note: How to Stay Fit for Life
modules.assignment: Write a 1-page Personal Wellness Manifesto covering fitness, nutrition, mental health, and sleep. Present a 12-month action plan with monthly checkpoints.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the five health-related components of physical fitness?
quizQuestions.answer: Agility
quizQuestions.explanation: The five health-related components are cardiovascular endurance, muscular strength, muscular endurance, flexibility, and body composition. Agility is a skill-related component.

quizQuestions.question: The ability of the heart and lungs to deliver oxygen to working muscles over a prolonged period is called:
quizQuestions.answer: Cardiovascular endurance
quizQuestions.explanation: Cardiovascular endurance reflects how efficiently the cardiorespiratory system sustains aerobic activity.

quizQuestions.question: Which fitness component measures the ratio of fat mass to lean mass?
quizQuestions.answer: Body composition
quizQuestions.explanation: Body composition describes the proportion of fat tissue versus lean tissue (muscle, bone, organs) in the body.

quizQuestions.question: Physical fitness is best described as:
options: The ability to run a marathon
A set of attributes relating to the ability to perform physical activity
quizQuestions.answer: A set of attributes relating to the ability to perform physical activity
quizQuestions.explanation: Physical fitness is multi-dimensional, encompassing multiple health- and skill-related components.

quizQuestions.question: Which component involves the ability of a muscle to exert maximum force against resistance in a single effort?
quizQuestions.answer: Muscular strength
quizQuestions.explanation: Muscular strength is the maximum force a muscle can produce in a single maximal effort.

quizQuestions.question: In the FITT principle, what does 'I' stand for?
quizQuestions.answer: Intensity
quizQuestions.explanation: FITT = Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type — the four variables used to design an exercise program.

quizQuestions.question: 'Frequency' in the FITT principle refers to:
options: How often you exercise per week
What type of activity you do
quizQuestions.answer: How often you exercise per week
quizQuestions.explanation: Frequency refers to the number of exercise sessions per week.

quizQuestions.question: Which FITT recommendation is most appropriate for a beginner wanting to improve cardiovascular fitness?
options: 5 days/week at maximum intensity for 60 minutes
3 days/week at moderate intensity for 30 minutes
1 day/week at high intensity for 90 minutes
7 days/week at low intensity for 10 minutes
quizQuestions.answer: 3 days/week at moderate intensity for 30 minutes
quizQuestions.explanation: Beginners should start with moderate frequency and intensity to build fitness safely and reduce injury risk.

quizQuestions.question: The 'Type' variable of FITT refers to:
options: The kind of physical activity performed
The time of day you exercise
quizQuestions.answer: The kind of physical activity performed
quizQuestions.explanation: Type specifies the mode of exercise (e.g., running, swimming, weightlifting) chosen to meet a fitness goal.

quizQuestions.question: The overload principle states that to improve fitness you must:
options: Gradually increase the demand placed on the body beyond its current capacity
quizQuestions.answer: Gradually increase the demand placed on the body beyond its current capacity
quizQuestions.explanation: Progressive overload means systematically increasing frequency, intensity, or time to continue producing fitness gains.

quizQuestions.question: VO2 max is a measure of:
options: Maximum oxygen consumption during intense exercise
quizQuestions.answer: Maximum oxygen consumption during intense exercise
quizQuestions.explanation: VO2 max represents the maximum rate at which the body can consume oxygen during maximal aerobic exercise.

quizQuestions.question: A lower resting heart rate generally indicates:
quizQuestions.answer: Better cardiovascular fitness
quizQuestions.explanation: Well-trained hearts pump more blood per beat (greater stroke volume), beating fewer times per minute at rest.

quizQuestions.question: Which activity is primarily aerobic?
quizQuestions.answer: 30-minute jog
quizQuestions.explanation: Aerobic activities are sustained, moderate-intensity efforts using oxygen as the primary energy source.

quizQuestions.question: The target heart rate zone for moderate aerobic exercise is approximately:
quizQuestions.answer: 50–70% of maximum heart rate
quizQuestions.explanation: Moderate-intensity aerobic exercise targets 50–70% of maximum heart rate, improving cardiovascular endurance safely.

quizQuestions.question: A simple estimate of maximum heart rate is:
quizQuestions.answer: 220 minus your age
quizQuestions.explanation: 220 - age provides an estimated maximum heart rate used to calculate training zones.

quizQuestions.question: Which test most directly measures muscular endurance?
quizQuestions.answer: Timed push-up or sit-up test
quizQuestions.explanation: Muscular endurance tests measure how many repetitions a muscle can perform over time, not maximum force.

quizQuestions.question: The principle of specificity in strength training means:
options: Train the same muscles every day
Training adaptations are specific to the muscles and movements trained
All exercises are equally effective for all muscles
Strength gains transfer automatically to all sports
quizQuestions.answer: Training adaptations are specific to the muscles and movements trained
quizQuestions.explanation: Specificity means the body adapts to the exact demands placed on it.

quizQuestions.question: Which muscle is primarily targeted by a push-up?
quizQuestions.answer: Pectoralis major
quizQuestions.explanation: Push-ups primarily work the pectoralis major (chest), assisted by the triceps and anterior deltoid.

quizQuestions.question: Hypertrophy refers to:
options: Increase in muscle fiber size due to training
quizQuestions.answer: Increase in muscle fiber size due to training
quizQuestions.explanation: Muscular hypertrophy is the enlargement of muscle fibers in response to resistance training.

quizQuestions.question: How many hours of rest between strength sessions for the same muscle group is recommended?
quizQuestions.answer: At least 24–48 hours
quizQuestions.explanation: Muscles need 24–48 hours to repair microtears and grow stronger before being retrained.

quizQuestions.question: Static stretching is best performed:
options: Before explosive athletic activity as a warm-up
After exercise when muscles are warm
First thing in the morning before any activity
quizQuestions.answer: After exercise when muscles are warm
quizQuestions.explanation: Static stretching is most effective and safest post-exercise when muscles are warmed up and pliable.

quizQuestions.question: Dynamic stretching involves:
options: Controlled movements through a full range of motion
A partner pushing your limb past its range
quizQuestions.answer: Controlled movements through a full range of motion
quizQuestions.explanation: Dynamic stretching uses controlled movements like leg swings and arm circles to prepare muscles for activity.

quizQuestions.question: The sit-and-reach test primarily measures flexibility of the:
quizQuestions.answer: Hamstrings and lower back
quizQuestions.explanation: The sit-and-reach test is widely used to assess hamstring and lower back flexibility.

quizQuestions.question: PNF stretching stands for:
quizQuestions.answer: Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation
quizQuestions.explanation: PNF uses alternating contraction and relaxation to achieve greater range of motion.

quizQuestions.question: Ballistic stretching is generally discouraged because it:
options: Uses bouncing movements that can trigger the stretch reflex and cause tears
Can only be done with a partner
quizQuestions.answer: Uses bouncing movements that can trigger the stretch reflex and cause tears
quizQuestions.explanation: Ballistic stretching's rapid bouncing can activate the stretch reflex and risk muscle injury.

quizQuestions.question: BMI is calculated as:
options: Fat mass divided by lean mass
quizQuestions.answer: Weight divided by height squared
quizQuestions.explanation: BMI = weight (kg) / height (m)^2. It is a simple screening proxy for body weight status.

quizQuestions.question: Which method provides the most accurate body fat percentage measurement?
quizQuestions.answer: Hydrostatic (underwater) weighing
quizQuestions.explanation: Hydrostatic weighing uses water displacement and Archimedes' principle to calculate body density and fat percentage accurately.

quizQuestions.question: Essential body fat is required for:
quizQuestions.answer: Normal physiological function
quizQuestions.explanation: Essential fat supports hormone production, organ protection, and other critical body functions.

quizQuestions.question: Lean body mass includes all of the following EXCEPT:
quizQuestions.answer: Adipose tissue
quizQuestions.explanation: Lean body mass includes everything except fat — muscle, bone, organs, and water.

quizQuestions.question: A key limitation of BMI is that it:
options: Does not account for fat distribution or muscle mass
quizQuestions.answer: Does not account for fat distribution or muscle mass
quizQuestions.explanation: BMI cannot distinguish fat from muscle, causing muscular athletes to be misclassified as overweight.

quizQuestions.question: The 'M' in SMART goals stands for:
quizQuestions.answer: Measurable
quizQuestions.explanation: SMART = Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Measurable means clear criteria exist for tracking progress.

quizQuestions.question: Which is the best example of a SMART fitness goal?
options: I will run a 5K in under 30 minutes within 8 weeks by training 4 days per week
I want to be healthy someday
quizQuestions.answer: I will run a 5K in under 30 minutes within 8 weeks by training 4 days per week
quizQuestions.explanation: This goal is specific (5K), measurable (under 30 min), achievable, relevant (fitness), and time-bound (8 weeks).

quizQuestions.question: Long-term fitness goals are typically set for:
options: Several months to a year or more
quizQuestions.answer: Several months to a year or more
quizQuestions.explanation: Long-term goals guide overall fitness direction and are supported by short-term milestone goals.

quizQuestions.question: Why is tracking progress important when pursuing a fitness goal?
options: To identify what is working and make evidence-based adjustments
To prove the goal was too easy
quizQuestions.answer: To identify what is working and make evidence-based adjustments
quizQuestions.explanation: Progress tracking provides data to recognize improvements and adjust the plan for continued success.

quizQuestions.question: The principle of reversibility means that:
options: Fitness gains are lost when training stops
Beginners improve faster than advanced athletes
quizQuestions.answer: Fitness gains are lost when training stops
quizQuestions.explanation: Reversibility ('use it or lose it') states the body returns toward pre-training state when exercise is discontinued.

quizQuestions.question: Carbohydrates are the preferred energy source for high-intensity exercise because they:
options: Are quickly broken down into glucose for immediate energy
Provide more energy per gram than fat
quizQuestions.answer: Are quickly broken down into glucose for immediate energy
quizQuestions.explanation: Carbohydrates are rapidly converted to glucose and glycogen, providing the primary fuel for high-intensity exercise.

quizQuestions.question: How much water should athletes generally drink before exercise?
quizQuestions.answer: 17–20 oz (500–600 mL) about 2 hours before
quizQuestions.explanation: Pre-hydration guidelines recommend 17–20 oz of water 2 hours before exercise for optimal hydration.

quizQuestions.question: Protein is most important for athletes in order to:
options: Support muscle repair and growth after exercise
quizQuestions.answer: Support muscle repair and growth after exercise
quizQuestions.explanation: Protein provides amino acids essential for repairing exercise-damaged muscle tissue and building new fibers.

quizQuestions.question: Glycogen is best defined as:
options: The stored form of carbohydrates in muscles and liver
A protein found in muscle fibers
quizQuestions.answer: The stored form of carbohydrates in muscles and liver
quizQuestions.explanation: Glycogen is synthesized from glucose and stored in skeletal muscle and liver as a readily accessible energy reserve.

quizQuestions.question: Signs of dehydration during exercise include all EXCEPT:
quizQuestions.answer: Increased energy levels
quizQuestions.explanation: Dehydration causes fatigue, reduced performance, dark urine, and dry mouth — not increased energy.

quizQuestions.question: The R.I.C.E. method stands for:
quizQuestions.answer: Rest, Ice, Compress, Elevate
quizQuestions.explanation: R.I.C.E. is the standard first-aid protocol for acute soft-tissue injuries to reduce swelling and pain.

quizQuestions.question: An acute sports injury is best described as:
options: An injury that develops gradually from overuse
A sudden injury from a specific traumatic event
quizQuestions.answer: A sudden injury from a specific traumatic event
quizQuestions.explanation: Acute injuries occur suddenly — such as a sprained ankle from a misstep or a fracture from a collision.

quizQuestions.question: DOMS typically peaks at:
quizQuestions.answer: 24–72 hours after intense exercise
quizQuestions.explanation: DOMS results from microscopic muscle damage and inflammation, typically peaking 24–72 hours after novel or intense exercise.

quizQuestions.question: A proper warm-up reduces injury risk by:
options: Raising muscle temperature and increasing blood flow
quizQuestions.answer: Raising muscle temperature and increasing blood flow
quizQuestions.explanation: Warm-up increases muscle temperature and circulation, making tissues more pliable and less prone to tearing.

quizQuestions.question: Shin splints are most commonly caused by:
options: Overuse and impact on hard surfaces with sudden training increases
quizQuestions.answer: Overuse and impact on hard surfaces with sudden training increases
quizQuestions.explanation: Shin splints (medial tibial stress syndrome) result from repetitive tibial stress, often when runners increase mileage too quickly.

quizQuestions.question: Sportsmanship is best described as:
options: Showing fairness, respect, and ethical behavior in sport
quizQuestions.answer: Showing fairness, respect, and ethical behavior in sport
quizQuestions.explanation: Good sportsmanship involves playing fairly, respecting opponents and officials, and accepting outcomes gracefully.

quizQuestions.question: In basketball, a 'carry' (palming) violation occurs when:
options: A player travels with the ball
A player turns their hand under the ball during dribbling
A player steps out of bounds
quizQuestions.answer: A player turns their hand under the ball during dribbling
quizQuestions.explanation: Carrying occurs when a player places their hand beneath the ball during dribbling, which is a violation.

quizQuestions.question: Effective team communication in sports includes:
options: Yelling at teammates who make mistakes
Calling out plays, using signals, and encouraging teammates
quizQuestions.answer: Calling out plays, using signals, and encouraging teammates
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective team communication involves clear verbal and nonverbal exchanges that coordinate play and support morale.

quizQuestions.question: Zone defense in basketball means:
options: Each player guards a specific opponent
Players guard geographic areas of the court
quizQuestions.answer: Players guard geographic areas of the court
quizQuestions.explanation: In zone defense, each defender covers a designated court area rather than following a specific player.

quizQuestions.question: Which is the best example of positive team leadership?
options: Encouraging peers and taking responsibility for your own performance
quizQuestions.answer: Encouraging peers and taking responsibility for your own performance
quizQuestions.explanation: Positive leaders support teammates, communicate constructively, and model accountability.

quizQuestions.question: A lifetime physical activity is one that:
options: Can only be done in high school
Can be enjoyed and sustained into older adulthood
Requires a professional coach at all times
quizQuestions.answer: Can be enjoyed and sustained into older adulthood
quizQuestions.explanation: Lifetime activities like swimming, golf, and cycling can be practiced safely and enjoyably throughout life.

quizQuestions.question: Which is considered a classic lifetime individual sport?
quizQuestions.answer: Swimming
quizQuestions.explanation: Swimming can be practiced safely by individuals of all ages, making it a quintessential lifetime activity.

quizQuestions.question: According to CDC guidelines, adults should aim for at least how much moderate aerobic activity per week?
quizQuestions.answer: 150 minutes
quizQuestions.explanation: CDC recommends at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week for adults.

quizQuestions.question: Which factor most supports sustained long-term physical activity participation?
options: Following a single rigid routine forever
quizQuestions.answer: Choosing activities you find enjoyable
quizQuestions.explanation: Enjoyment is the strongest predictor of long-term exercise adherence.

quizQuestions.question: Yoga qualifies as a lifetime activity primarily because it:
options: Can be adapted to any fitness level and age
quizQuestions.answer: Can be adapted to any fitness level and age
quizQuestions.explanation: Yoga's adaptability in intensity, duration, and style makes it accessible and beneficial across all life stages.

quizQuestions.question: Regular physical activity is associated with which mental health benefit?
options: Reduced symptoms of anxiety and depression
quizQuestions.answer: Reduced symptoms of anxiety and depression
quizQuestions.explanation: Exercise triggers endorphin release and neurochemical changes that reduce anxiety and depression.

quizQuestions.question: The 'runner's high' is linked to the release of:
quizQuestions.answer: Endorphins and endocannabinoids
quizQuestions.explanation: The runner's high is a feeling of euphoria linked to endorphin and endocannabinoid release during sustained aerobic exercise.

quizQuestions.question: Exercise helps manage stress by:
options: Lowering cortisol over time and improving mood-related neurotransmitters
quizQuestions.answer: Lowering cortisol over time and improving mood-related neurotransmitters
quizQuestions.explanation: Regular exercise regulates cortisol and boosts serotonin and dopamine, improving mood and stress resilience.

quizQuestions.question: Which type of exercise has the strongest evidence for reducing anxiety symptoms?
quizQuestions.answer: Aerobic exercise
quizQuestions.explanation: Aerobic exercise is most strongly linked to anxiety reduction through endorphin and serotonin activity.

quizQuestions.question: The 'Exercise is Medicine' concept refers to:
options: Replacing all medical treatments with workouts
Using physical activity as a strategy to prevent and manage disease and mental health conditions
Only professional athletes using fitness therapy
quizQuestions.answer: Using physical activity as a strategy to prevent and manage disease and mental health conditions
quizQuestions.explanation: Exercise is Medicine is a global initiative promoting physical activity as a vital component of disease prevention and care.

quizQuestions.question: The FitnessGram PACER test measures:
quizQuestions.answer: Aerobic capacity
quizQuestions.explanation: The PACER (Progressive Aerobic Cardiovascular Endurance Run) is a multistage aerobic test estimating VO2 max.

quizQuestions.question: Comparing fitness test results to norms helps to:
options: Determine whether scores fall within a healthy range for age and gender
quizQuestions.answer: Determine whether scores fall within a healthy range for age and gender
quizQuestions.explanation: Fitness norms provide criterion-referenced healthy fitness zones for age and gender groups.

quizQuestions.question: Why use multiple fitness tests rather than just one?
options: Because fitness is multi-dimensional and no single test measures all components
One test is always more accurate
quizQuestions.answer: Because fitness is multi-dimensional and no single test measures all components
quizQuestions.explanation: A battery of tests gives a complete picture across cardiovascular, muscular, flexibility, and body composition dimensions.

quizQuestions.question: To track fitness progress reliably, assessments should be:
options: Conducted under consistent conditions at regular intervals
Changed each time to prevent adaptation
Done only when feeling at your best
quizQuestions.answer: Conducted under consistent conditions at regular intervals
quizQuestions.explanation: Standardized conditions (same protocol, time, equipment) ensure score changes reflect true fitness changes.

quizQuestions.question: Which data point best indicates improvement in cardiovascular fitness over 6 weeks?
quizQuestions.answer: Decrease in mile run time
quizQuestions.explanation: A faster mile run time indicates improved aerobic capacity and cardiovascular efficiency.

quizQuestions.question: A sustainable wellness lifestyle is best characterized by:
options: Extreme diets and maximum training load
Balanced habits in fitness, nutrition, sleep, and mental health maintained over time
quizQuestions.answer: Balanced habits in fitness, nutrition, sleep, and mental health maintained over time
quizQuestions.explanation: Sustainable wellness integrates manageable, enjoyable habits across multiple health dimensions for long-term wellbeing.

quizQuestions.question: Sleep is important for fitness because:
options: It burns the most calories of any activity
Growth hormone peaks during deep sleep, driving muscle repair
It replaces the need for aerobic exercise
Sleep has no effect on performance
quizQuestions.answer: Growth hormone peaks during deep sleep, driving muscle repair
quizQuestions.explanation: During slow-wave sleep, growth hormone secretion peaks and muscle repair and consolidation occur.

quizQuestions.question: Which behavior most supports long-term fitness adherence?
options: Setting unrealistic goals for rapid transformation
Exercising with friends or a community for accountability
Doing only high-intensity exercise every day
quizQuestions.answer: Exercising with friends or a community for accountability
quizQuestions.explanation: Social support and accountability through exercise partners or groups significantly improve long-term adherence.

quizQuestions.question: The wellness wheel typically includes dimensions such as:
options: Physical, emotional, social, intellectual, spiritual, and environmental
Financial wealth and career success only
quizQuestions.answer: Physical, emotional, social, intellectual, spiritual, and environmental
quizQuestions.explanation: The wellness wheel encompasses multiple interconnected dimensions of health beyond physical fitness.

quizQuestions.question: A personal wellness plan is most effective when it:
options: Is tailored to individual goals, preferences, and life circumstances
Is set once and never revisited
quizQuestions.answer: Is tailored to individual goals, preferences, and life circumstances
quizQuestions.explanation: Individualized plans reflecting personal values, schedules, and interests are more motivating and sustainable.

questions.question: Which of the following is a health-related component of physical fitness?
questions.answer: Cardiovascular endurance
questions.explanation: Health-related components: cardiovascular endurance, muscular strength, muscular endurance, flexibility, body composition.

questions.question: In the FITT principle, 'Time' refers to:
options: The hour of day you exercise
questions.answer: The duration of each session
questions.explanation: Time in FITT refers to how long each individual workout session lasts.

questions.question: A student performs 3 sets of 15 push-ups. This primarily develops:
questions.answer: Muscular endurance
questions.explanation: High-repetition, lower-resistance exercises primarily develop muscular endurance.

questions.question: Maximum heart rate is estimated by subtracting your age from:
questions.explanation: The formula 220 - age is the standard estimation of maximum heart rate.

questions.question: Dynamic stretching differs from static stretching because dynamic stretching:
options: Uses controlled movement through a range of motion
questions.answer: Uses controlled movement through a range of motion
questions.explanation: Dynamic stretching involves purposeful movement, unlike static stretching which holds a fixed position.

questions.question: BMI measures:
questions.answer: Weight relative to height squared
questions.explanation: BMI = weight (kg) / height (m)^2 — a screening proxy for body weight status.

questions.question: The overload principle states that to improve fitness you must:
options: Stress the body beyond its current level to produce adaptation
Keep all workouts the same intensity
Train at maximum intensity from day one
questions.answer: Stress the body beyond its current level to produce adaptation
questions.explanation: Progressive overload — gradually increasing demand — is the foundation of fitness improvement.

questions.question: The body's preferred fuel for high-intensity exercise is:
questions.answer: Carbohydrate
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates are rapidly metabolized to glucose, the primary fuel for high-intensity exercise.

questions.question: R.I.C.E. is applied immediately after:
options: An acute soft-tissue injury such as a sprain
questions.answer: An acute soft-tissue injury such as a sprain
questions.explanation: R.I.C.E. (Rest, Ice, Compress, Elevate) is immediate first aid for acute soft-tissue injuries.

questions.question: Sportsmanship includes which behavior?
options: Celebrating aggressively in an opponent's face
questions.answer: Acknowledging good play by opponents
questions.explanation: Good sportsmanship includes respect for opponents, officials, and the game.

questions.question: The sit-and-reach assessment tests:
questions.answer: Flexibility
questions.explanation: The sit-and-reach test measures flexibility, primarily of the hamstrings and lower back.

questions.question: SMART goals help athletes because they:
options: Provide clear, measurable direction and timelines
questions.answer: Provide clear, measurable direction and timelines
questions.explanation: SMART goals create accountability and clarity, making it easier to track progress and stay motivated.

questions.question: VO2 max measures:
options: Maximum oxygen uptake during intense aerobic exercise
questions.answer: Maximum oxygen uptake during intense aerobic exercise
questions.explanation: VO2 max is the gold-standard measure of aerobic fitness.

questions.question: Muscular hypertrophy refers to:
options: Increased size of muscle fibers due to training
questions.answer: Increased size of muscle fibers due to training
questions.explanation: Hypertrophy is the enlargement of existing muscle fibers in response to resistance training.

questions.question: Which activity best develops cardiovascular endurance?
questions.answer: 45-minute cycling session
questions.explanation: Sustained aerobic activities like 45-minute cycling best develop cardiovascular endurance.

questions.question: The principle of specificity means that:
options: All exercises improve all fitness components equally
Training adaptations are specific to the exercise type performed
questions.answer: Training adaptations are specific to the exercise type performed
questions.explanation: The body adapts to the exact type, intensity, and movement pattern of exercise applied.

questions.question: DOMS typically occurs:
questions.answer: 24–72 hours after exercise
questions.explanation: DOMS results from micro-damage and inflammation in muscle fibers, peaking 1–3 days after intense or unaccustomed exercise.

questions.question: Essential body fat is necessary for:
options: Normal physiological functions including hormone production and organ protection
questions.answer: Normal physiological functions including hormone production and organ protection
questions.explanation: Essential fat supports critical body functions and cannot be eliminated without serious health consequences.

questions.question: Regular aerobic exercise benefits mental health primarily by:
options: Increasing endorphins and regulating stress hormones
questions.answer: Increasing endorphins and regulating stress hormones
questions.explanation: Aerobic exercise triggers endorphin release and lowers chronic cortisol, reducing anxiety and depression.

questions.question: A lifetime physical activity:
options: Can be enjoyed throughout adulthood regardless of age
questions.answer: Can be enjoyed throughout adulthood regardless of age
questions.explanation: Lifetime activities like swimming, cycling, and yoga can be adapted and sustained across all life stages.

questions.question: Sleep is crucial for recovery because:
options: It eliminates the need to train
Growth hormone peaks during deep sleep, promoting muscle repair
Sleep burns more calories than exercise
questions.answer: Growth hormone peaks during deep sleep, promoting muscle repair
questions.explanation: Most growth hormone secretion and muscle repair occur during slow-wave (deep) sleep.

questions.question: Glycogen stores are primarily depleted during:
questions.answer: Prolonged moderate-to-high intensity exercise
questions.explanation: Glycogen is the primary fuel for moderate-to-high intensity exercise and becomes significantly depleted during prolonged sessions.

questions.question: The most important injury prevention strategy before explosive athletic activity is:
options: A dynamic warm-up including movement preparation
questions.answer: A dynamic warm-up including movement preparation
questions.explanation: Dynamic warm-ups increase heart rate, blood flow, and muscle temperature while activating neuromuscular patterns needed for sport.

questions.question: The FitnessGram PACER test assesses:
questions.answer: Aerobic capacity
questions.explanation: The PACER is a multistage field test estimating aerobic capacity.

questions.question: Which is the best SMART fitness goal?
options: Complete 10 more push-ups than my current max within 4 weeks, training 3 days/week
questions.answer: Complete 10 more push-ups than my current max within 4 weeks, training 3 days/week
questions.explanation: This goal is Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound.

questions.question: The reversibility principle states that:
options: Fitness gains decline when training stops
Training effects reverse after one missed session
Only beginners lose fitness when inactive
questions.answer: Fitness gains decline when training stops
questions.explanation: 'Use it or lose it' — fitness adaptations regress toward baseline when training is discontinued.

questions.question: Dehydration during exercise causes:
options: Decreased performance, heat illness risk, and impaired cognition
questions.answer: Decreased performance, heat illness risk, and impaired cognition
questions.explanation: Even mild dehydration (2% body weight loss) reduces aerobic performance and increases heat illness risk.

questions.question: A holistic wellness lifestyle attends to:
options: Physical, mental, social, and emotional health dimensions
questions.answer: Physical, mental, social, and emotional health dimensions
questions.explanation: Holistic wellness addresses multiple interconnected dimensions for comprehensive health and quality of life.

questions.question: PNF stretching achieves greater range of motion by:
options: Using alternating muscle contraction and relaxation
questions.answer: Using alternating muscle contraction and relaxation
questions.explanation: PNF exploits the neurological stretch reflex — alternating contraction and relaxation allows muscles to lengthen further.

questions.question: Team sports develop which skill beyond physical fitness?
questions.answer: Communication, collaboration, and leadership
questions.explanation: Team sports develop interpersonal skills alongside physical abilities.

questions.question: A cool-down after exercise is important because it:
options: Gradually reduces heart rate and helps remove metabolic waste
questions.answer: Gradually reduces heart rate and helps remove metabolic waste
questions.explanation: Cool-downs let the cardiovascular system transition safely to rest and help clear lactic acid from muscles.

questions.question: The best assessment of muscular strength is:
questions.answer: One-repetition maximum (1RM) test
questions.explanation: The 1RM test measures the maximum weight a person can lift for one repetition, directly assessing maximal strength.

questions.question: Recommended sleep duration for teenagers is:
questions.answer: 8–10 hours per night
questions.explanation: Adolescents need 8–10 hours to support growth, recovery, cognitive function, and overall health.

questions.question: Which is an example of an aerobic activity?
questions.answer: 30-minute dance class
questions.explanation: Aerobic activities are sustained, moderate-intensity exercises relying on oxygen. A 30-minute dance class qualifies.

questions.question: Shin splints are best prevented by:
options: Gradually increasing load, wearing proper footwear, and cross-training
questions.answer: Gradually increasing load, wearing proper footwear, and cross-training
questions.explanation: Prevention includes the 10% rule (increase weekly mileage by no more than 10%), supportive shoes, and varied training surfaces.
